gates.name: protocol conformance
gates.detail: random: 100/100 self-games, 0 protocol errors / timeouts / illegal moves.

gates.name: warm-up isolation
gates.detail: NPS flat across moves 1-20; no move-1 cold-start spike.

gates.name: language tax (miniature)
gates.detail: cpp-alphabeta vs py-alphabeta (same algorithm): score share 48% fixed-node -> 72% wall-clock. Speed ratio 30.7x (4,059,694 vs 132,183 nps).

contracts.label: No random-mover team wins the Cup
contracts.desc: Resolves YES if no team backed by the random engine lifts the trophy.

contracts.label: A Python-language team reaches the Final
contracts.desc: Resolves YES if at least one Python-backed team plays in the Final.

contracts.label: An alpha-beta engine wins the Cup
contracts.desc: The language tax is real: resolves YES if the champion is an alpha-beta search engine (any language).

contracts.label: A C++ team wins the Cup
contracts.desc: Resolves YES if the champion is backed by a C++ engine.

contracts.label: Argentina retains the title
contracts.desc: Resolves YES if Argentina wins the 2026 World Cup.

contracts.label: A team outside pot 1 wins the Cup
contracts.desc: Resolves YES if the champion was not a pot-1 seed.

contracts.label: A CONMEBOL team wins the Cup
contracts.desc: Resolves YES if a South American (CONMEBOL) side lifts the trophy.

contracts.label: An all-C++ Final
contracts.desc: Resolves YES if both teams in the Final are backed by a C++ engine.

engine_info.blurb: Classical alpha-beta minimax with pruning, compiled straight to the metal. The speed pole of the field: whatever the algorithm can do per node, C++ does it fastest.
engine_info.name: cpp-alphabeta

engine_info.blurb: The exact same alpha-beta, written in Rust and calling the C core over FFI. Memory-safe and within a hair of C++ speed — proof the tax is about the language runtime, not the algorithm.
engine_info.name: rs-alphabeta

engine_info.blurb: Alpha-beta in Node.js, with the perft-clean movegen compiled to WebAssembly. The hot path runs in WASM, so the web platform hangs surprisingly close to the compiled pack.
engine_info.name: js-alphabeta

engine_info.blurb: The same search again, in clean idiomatic Python via ctypes. Elegant and easy to read — and it pays the full interpreter tax, ~20-30x fewer nodes/sec than C++. The headline of the whole experiment.
engine_info.name: py-alphabeta

engine_info.blurb: A completely different paradigm: instead of exhaustively pruning a tree, it grows one by sampling promising lines (UCT) and backing up the results. The knowledge pole — it must win on Elo-per-node, not raw speed.
engine_info.name: py-mcts

engine_info.blurb: Textbook minimax with the pruning switched OFF — it dutifully visits every branch to its depth. It exists to show why alpha-beta matters: the same move, at a fraction of the work, once you prune.
engine_info.name: py-minimax

engine_info.blurb: One ply deep: it plays whatever move maximises the eval right now, with zero foresight. Fast, shallow, and easily out-planned — but it'll punish a free piece instantly.
engine_info.name: cpp-greedy

engine_info.blurb: Depth-1 greedy, Python edition. The lightest search there is short of not searching at all.
engine_info.name: py-greedy

engine_info.blurb: No evaluation, no search — a uniform-random legal move. The Elo anchor of the tournament and the protocol canary: if anything speaks UCI, it's this.
engine_info.name: random

engine_info.blurb: Not a competitor — the reference analyst. It runs the strongest search in the project (alpha-beta with a transposition table and PVS) at a high budget, so its evaluation and best line are the ground truth used to annotate every game in the Watch view.
engine_info.name: cpp-analyst
